sections.title: The big contrast — finished vs connected to now
sections.content:
Two tenses, one of the highest-stakes choices in English. The question is never "which event happened?" but "how does the speaker frame it?"

- **Past simple** = action in a **finished time** — over, separate from now: *I lived in Prague in 2020.*
- **Present perfect** = action **connected to now** — the experience or its result still matters: *I have lived in Prague* (and the experience is part of who I am today).

**Why it matters (roots-first)**: This is the heart of the Verb Phrase root (#2 in the Tree Model). The perfect aspect is English's main grammatical marker of **result and present relevance** — and Czech has no direct equivalent. Czech *byl jsem v Praze* covers both *I was in Prague* (finished) and *I have been to Prague* (experience). English forces the speaker to choose. Getting this contrast right is the single biggest tense-confusion fix for Czech learners — and it shapes how you sound in job interviews, exams, CVs and everyday speech.

**Same words, different frames**:
- *I worked at Skanska for three years.* (finished — I don't work there now)
- *I have worked at Skanska for three years.* (still working there now)

diagram.title: Past simple vs present perfect — at a glance
rows: *I have gone, she has finished, they have seen*
ever, never, just, already, yet, since, for
*I have seen him* (at some point — relevant now).*

sections.title: Time markers — the decisive clue
sections.content:
In about 90% of exam gap-fills (and most real speech), the **time marker** tells you which tense to use. Learn the two lists side by side and you have most of the topic solved.

**Why it matters**: A single time word locks the tense. *Yesterday* forbids present perfect; *since 2020* forbids past simple. This is the fastest, most reliable rule you'll meet in English grammar.

diagram.title: Time markers — past simple vs present perfect
rows: **so far, up to now, recently, lately**

sections.title: News and recent results — the conversation shift
sections.content:
**Present perfect breaks news.** **Past simple gives details.** Listen for the switch — it happens constantly in real conversation.

A typical exchange:
- A: *I've broken my leg!* (present perfect — the news, the result is now)
- B: *Oh no — how did it happen?* (past simple — now we're asking about the finished moment)
- A: *I fell off my bike yesterday.* (past simple — finished time stated)

**Why it matters**: This pattern is everywhere — work updates, accidents, achievements, gossip. The first sentence announces (*She's got the job! / The boss has just left / I've finished the report*); follow-up questions and details slide straight into past simple. Mixing them up sounds odd and slows the listener down.

**More contrast pairs**:
- *I've lost my wallet.* (now — I don't have it) → *I lost it on the metro this morning.* (details, finished time)
- *She has won the contract!* (news, result now) → *She signed it yesterday.* (specific past moment)

sections.title: Life experience vs specific past — the classic trap
sections.content:
When you ask about someone's **life so far**, with no specific time in mind, English uses **present perfect**. As soon as you mention **a particular time**, switch to **past simple**.

**Side-by-side**:
- *Have you ever been to London?* (any time in your life? — experience)
- *Did you go to London last summer?* (specific finished time — fact)

- *I've met three British people.* (in my life, so far)
- *I met three British people at the conference.* (at that finished event)

- *She has read a lot of Russian novels.* (life experience, ongoing relevance)
- *She read War and Peace last winter.* (finished, time stated)

**Why it matters**: This is the #1 trap in speaking exams and job interviews. *Have you ever taught children?* — *Yes, I have. I taught at a primary school in 2018.* The interviewer expects the present perfect for the experience question and the past simple for the example. Czech translates both with the same past — English needs the switch.

sections.title: Since & for — present perfect's exclusive territory
sections.content:
For a state or action that **started in the past and is still going on now**, English uses **present perfect** — never present simple. This is one of the strongest Czech-interference points.

- **since** + a starting point: *since 2020, since Monday, since I was a child*
- **for** + a duration: *for three years, for ages, for a long time*

**Czech reflex vs English rule**:
- Wrong: **I am living* here for three years.* (Czech literal: *bydlím tady tři roky*)
- Wrong: **I live* here since 2020.*
- Right: *I **have lived** here for three years.* / *I **have lived** here since 2020.*
- Right (continuous, often more natural for actions): *I **have been living** here since 2020.*

**Why it matters**: This appears in every interview, every CV, every "tell me about yourself". *I've worked at TAXVIA for four years. I've been studying English since 2019.* Getting *since* and *for* into present perfect is the single most professional-sounding fix for a Czech intermediate speaker.

**Quick rule**: *since* + point in time; *for* + length of time. Both demand present perfect (simple or continuous).

sections.title: Quick note — simple vs continuous
sections.content:
Present perfect itself splits into **simple** and **continuous** — a fine distinction, treated fully in its own topic.

- **Present perfect simple** (*I have worked*) — emphasises **result, completion, or quantity**: *I've written three reports today.*
- **Present perfect continuous** (*I have been working*) — emphasises **duration or ongoing activity**: *I've been writing reports all morning.*

**Quick contrast**:
- *I've read that book.* (finished it — the result)
- *I've been reading that book.* (in progress — still going)

**Why it matters**: For past-simple-vs-present-perfect choices, either form usually works — the simple/continuous split is a second-level decision. Get the **tense** right first; refine to continuous when you want to highlight duration or ongoing process.

**Full treatment**: see the separate **Present Perfect (Simple vs Continuous)** topic.

sections.title: Common Czech-speaker errors
sections.content:
The high-frequency mistakes — all driven by Czech having one past tense where English has two.

**1. Present perfect with a finished-time marker**
- Wrong: *I have been in Prague yesterday.*
- Right: *I was in Prague yesterday.*
- Rule: *yesterday* = finished time → past simple, never present perfect.

**2. "When?" with present perfect**
- Wrong: *When have you arrived?*
- Right: *When did you arrive?*
- Rule: *When?* asks for a specific point in time → past simple.

**3. Present simple with *since* / *for* (Czech literal translation)**
- Wrong: *I live here since 2020.* (Czech: *bydlím tady od 2020*)
- Right: *I have lived here since 2020.*
- Rule: started in past + still true now → present perfect.

**4. Past simple where present perfect's needed (experience)**
- Wrong: *Did you ever try sushi?* (asking about life experience)
- Right: *Have you ever tried sushi?*
- Rule: *ever / never* about life experience → present perfect.

**5. Past simple where the result matters now**
- Wrong: *I lost my keys.* (said when explaining why you can't get in right now)
- Right: *I've lost my keys.*
- Rule: result still relevant → present perfect.

**Why it matters**: These five errors account for the vast majority of tense mistakes Czech speakers make at B1–B2. Fix them and your English immediately sounds more native — especially in job interviews, where you're constantly moving between life experience (*I have worked, I have studied*) and specific past events (*I started there in 2020, I finished my degree last year*).

questions.question: Which sentence is correct?
questions.explanation: Yesterday is a finished past time → past simple.

questions.question: We want to ask about life experience (no specific time). Which is natural?

options.a: Did you ever go to Paris?
options.b: Do you ever go to Paris?
options.c: Have you ever been to Paris?

questions.question: She moved here in 2020 and still lives here. Which is correct?
questions.explanation: Since + duration up to now → present perfect.

questions.question: We want to know when something happened. Which is natural?

mc.title: Practice: Past simple vs Present perfect (multiple choice)

options.a: I have been to the cinema yesterday.
options.b: I went to the cinema yesterday.
options.c: I have gone to the cinema yesterday.
options.d: I go to the cinema yesterday.

questions.question: 'She ____ last month.' (She left and is gone.) Which fits?
questions.explanation: Last month = finished time → past simple.

questions.question: 'We ____ here for five years.' (We still live here.) Which fits?
questions.explanation: For + duration up to now → present perfect.

questions.question: Which is wrong?

options.a: When did you start your job?
options.b: Have you ever been to Japan?

questions.explanation: Yesterday → past simple, not present perfect.

questions.question: 'Just' (recently, result now) usually takes:
questions.explanation: Just (recent past, result now) → present perfect.

gapfill.title: Practice: Gap fill (Past simple vs Present perfect)

questions.question: I ____ (see) that film last week.
answers: saw
questions.explanation: Last week = finished past time → past simple.

questions.question: She ____ (live) in London for three years. (She still lives there.)
answers: has lived
questions.explanation: For + duration up to now → present perfect.

questions.question: ____ you ever ____ (be) to Japan?
answers: have you ever been

questions.question: They ____ (go) home an hour ago.
answers: went
questions.explanation: Ago = finished past time → past simple.

questions.question: I ____ (just / finish) my homework.
answers: have just finished
have just finished my homework

questions.question: When ____ you ____ (start) learning English?
answers: did you start

errorcorrection.title: Practice: Error correction (Past simple vs Present perfect)

questions.question: Wrong: 'I have been to the cinema yesterday.' Correct the sentence.
answers: i went to the cinema yesterday
I went to the cinema yesterday.
I went to the cinema yesterday

questions.question: Wrong: 'She has left last month.' Correct the sentence.
answers: she left last month
She left last month.
She left last month

questions.question: Wrong: 'Did you ever try Indian food?' (asking about life experience) Correct the sentence.
answers: have you ever tried indian food
Have you ever tried Indian food?
Have you ever tried Indian food

makesentence.title: Practice: Making sentences (Past simple vs Present perfect)

answers: I saw the film last week.
I saw a film last week.

questions.prompt: she / live / here / five years (still lives here)
answers: She has lived here for five years.
She's lived here for five years.
questions.explanation: Duration up to now → present perfect.

questions.prompt: you / ever / be / New York (form the question)
answers: Have you ever been to New York?
Have you ever been to New York
questions.explanation: Life experience question: Have you ever been to ...? (ever with present perfect).

answers: They have just arrived.
They've just arrived.

makequestion.title: Practice: Making questions (Past simple vs Present perfect)

answers: When did you start your job?
When did you start the job?
Have they ever been to Australia?
Have they ever been to Australia
How long has she had the car?
How long has she had her car?
questions.explanation: How long (duration up to now) → present perfect.
